g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Public Key Infrastructures, the foundation of modern digital security.
PKI is a framework of roles, policies, hardware and software that creates secure communication using digital certificates.
g.text: PKI is a set of policies, procedures, and technologies used to manage
public-private key pairs and digital certificates for secure communication.

g.data-narration: PKI enables authentication, encryption, and digital signatures, making it essential for secure web browsing, email, and VPNs.
A PKI system comprises four main components: Certificate Authority, Registration Authority, Certificate Repository, and Key Generator.
The Certificate Authority (CA) is a trusted third party that issues digital certificates after verifying the applicant's identity.
g.text: • A trusted entity that issues digital certificates
• Verifies identity of applicants before issuing certificates
• Binds the public key to the identity of the certificate holder

g.data-narration: The Registration Authority (RA) acts as an intermediary that verifies the identity of certificate requestors before forwarding requests to the CA.
g.text: • An entity that verifies the identity of the applicant
• Acts as an intermediary between applicant and CA
• Forwards verified requests to the CA for certificate issuance

g.data-narration: The Certificate Repository stores and publishes certificates, while the Private Key Generator creates secure key pairs for encryption.
g.text: • A database that stores digital certificates

g.data-narration: The PKI workflow begins with key pair generation, where the applicant creates a public-private key pair using secure software or hardware.
g.text: Working of PKI: Step 1 - Key Pair Generation

g.data-narration: The applicant submits a certificate request to the RA, which verifies identity and forwards the request to the CA for certificate issuance.
g.text: Working of PKI: Steps 2 & 3 - Certificate Request & Issuance

g.data-narration: After issuance, the certificate is deployed to the applicant's system. When communication occurs, the receiving party verifies the certificate.
g.text: Working of PKI: Steps 4 & 5 - Deployment & Verification
✓ Signature valid ✓ Not expired ✓ Not revoked

g.data-narration: X.509 is the standard format for public key certificates, containing the subject's identity, public key, validity period, and CA signature.
g.text: X.509 is a standard format for public key certificates that contains:

g.data-narration: PKI has widespread applications in secure web browsing, email encryption, VPNs, and digital signatures for enterprise security.
PKI secures HTTPS connections, encrypts emails using S/MIME, authenticates VPN users, and ensures document integrity through digital signatures.
g.text: • HTTPS: Authenticate websites and encrypt data
• S/MIME: Encrypt and sign email messages

g.data-narration: In summary, PKI provides authentication, encryption, and digital signatures through CAs, RAs, repositories, and key generators.
g.text: • PKI manages public-private key pairs and digital certificates
• CA issues certificates, RA verifies identity, Repository stores certificates
• X.509 is the standard certificate format with subject, public key, validity
• PKI enables secure web, email encryption, VPNs, and digital signatures

g.data-narration: For exams, remember to explain PKI components, describe the certificate lifecycle, and compare symmetric vs asymmetric key distribution methods.
g.text: 1. Explain PKI components and their roles 2. Describe the certificate lifecycle 3. Compare symmetric vs asymmetric key distribution
4. Discuss X.509 certificate structure 5. Explain PKI management challenges 6. Know the applications of PKI
